attrs.alt: Design Change Control Flow After Sign-Off

content.text: Why Change Control Matters After Sign-Off

Design sign-off is not a gate that closes and never opens again — it is a snapshot of the team's current understanding of the feature's scope, safety, and feasibility. After sign-off, reality intervenes: an engineering constraint surfaces, a safety finding changes the required refusal conditions, a legal requirement shifts, or a model provider updates their API. Each of these events may require a change to the approved design.

Without a change control process, post-sign-off changes happen informally. The product manager makes a quick scope adjustment during a standup. An engineer tweaks the system prompt to fix a bug without updating the approved hash. Legal sends a comment via email that nobody tracks to a formal change. The result is that the production system drifts from the approved design, and nobody knows which approved version is actually running.

The design change control process answers two questions: who is authorised to approve a given type of change, and how is the change logged so that the audit trail remains intact. The process must be fast enough to not block engineering velocity, yet rigorous enough to prevent safety and compliance drift.
The Four Change Classes
Minor Changes

A minor change is a wording or formatting adjustment that does not shift the semantic meaning of any scope, safety, or tone directive. Rephrasing a refusal message from 'I cannot help with that' to 'That is outside my area — you might try [resource]' is a minor change. The agent's behaviour in that scenario has not changed; only the wording of the output has changed.
Approval path:
Product Manager only, within four business hours. The PM reviews the change against the approved system prompt, confirms there is no semantic shift, and records the approval in the change log.
Eval suite requirement:

run the eval suite after every minor change before deploying. Minor changes can have non-obvious effects on other parts of the agent's behaviour because the model uses the full prompt context when generating responses.
No hash update required:
if the change is a formatting fix with no semantic content change, the hash does not need to be updated in the sign-off RACI. If there is any semantic content, update the hash.
Major Changes

A major change is one that shifts the agent's scope, tone, tool calls, or failure handling behaviour. Adding a new capability (such as enabling the agent to book appointments when it previously could only look up availability) is a major change. Changing the agent's response language from formal to casual is a major change. Adding a new tool call to an external API is a major change because it introduces a new failure surface.
Approval path:

all domain accountables whose domains are impacted by the change must re-sign within 48 business hours. If the change adds a new tool call, the Engineering Lead and Safety Reviewer must re-sign. If the change shifts tone, the Design Lead re-signs.
Prompt re-hash:
every major change results in a new prompt hash. The new hash is recorded in the RACI and the change log. The old hash is retained in the version history but marked as superseded.
Partial re-review:

major changes do not require a full six-domain re-review. Only the affected domains are reviewed. However, if a reviewer of an unaffected domain believes the change has cross-domain implications, they can request a broader review.
Safety Changes

A safety change is any modification that adds, removes, or modifies a harm category, a refusal condition, a human-in-the-loop threshold, or a safety guardrail. Safety changes have a 24-hour SLA because they are time-sensitive (a safety deficiency in a live system should be addressed within one day) but also require careful review.
Safety Reviewer is Accountable:

the Safety Reviewer has final authority on safety changes. The PM cannot override a safety change rejection, and the Engineering Lead cannot deploy a safety change without the Safety Reviewer's explicit approval.
Legal is Consulted:

safety changes often have legal implications. The Legal and Compliance reviewer must be consulted (not just informed) for every safety change. Their input may not change the safety decision, but it must be sought.
Eval suite rerun mandatory:

every safety change requires a full eval suite run across all harm categories, not just the category affected by the change. Safety changes can have unexpected interactions with other safety conditions.
Emergency Changes

An emergency change is required when active harm is detected in production — a prompt injection attack is succeeding, the agent is providing harmful outputs, or a safety guardrail has failed. The emergency classification allows the Eng Lead or Safety Reviewer to authorise an immediate rollback or hotfix without waiting for the normal approval chain.
Rollback first:

the first response to an emergency is always to roll back to the last known-good prompt hash. Attempting to fix a production safety issue with a new prompt while the harmful behaviour is active is slower and riskier than rolling back first.
Retroactive sign-off:
the emergency change is made first; all other domain sign-offs are collected retroactively within 24 hours. This prevents the approval process from slowing the response to active harm.
Incident report required:

every emergency change triggers an incident report that documents what happened, when it was detected, what the change was, and what the root cause was. This report is stored alongside the change log entry.
The Change Log

The change log is a persistent record of every post-sign-off modification. Its primary purpose is audit: if a production issue is traced to a specific prompt change, the log provides the identity of the approver, the date, and the motivation for the change.

# design-change-log.md
# Log every post-sign-off change BEFORE making it.
# Fields: type | date | requestor | impacted domains | change summary | new hash | approver | SLA met

| # | Type      | Date       | Requestor   | Impacted Domains       | Summary                               | New Hash | Approver  | SLA Met |
|---|-----------|------------|-------------|------------------------|---------------------------------------|----------|-----------|---------|
| 1 | MINOR     | 2025-02-01 | PM          | None                   | Reworded "I cannot help" to softer    | b7e901cc | PM        | Yes     |
| 2 | MAJOR     | 2025-02-03 | Eng Lead    | UX, Technical          | Added streaming response mode         | 9d34aa12 | PM+Eng    | Yes     |
| 3 | SAFETY    | 2025-02-05 | Safety      | Safety, Legal          | Restrict financial advice scope       | 2c78f55e | Safety+PM | Yes     |
| 4 | MINOR     | 2025-02-07 | Design      | None                   | Shortened clarification phrasing      | f1c390ad | PM        | Yes     |
| 5 | EMERGENCY | 2025-02-09 | Eng Lead    | Safety                 | Hotfix prompt injection vulnerability | 44ab0011 | Safety    | Yes     |

## Change Classification Guide
Minor:     Wording or formatting adjustment; no semantic shift in scope, tone, or safety.
           Examples: rephrasing a refusal, shortening a clarification question.
           Approval: PM only. SLA: 4 business hours.

Major:     Scope change, tone change, new tool call, new persona, new failure path.
           Examples: adding a booking capability, changing language register.
           Approval: all affected domain accountables. SLA: 48 business hours.

Safety:    Change that adds, removes, or modifies a harm category or safety guardrail.
           Examples: changing a refusal condition, modifying HITL threshold.
           Approval: Safety Reviewer (A) + Legal (C). SLA: 24 business hours.

Emergency: Active harm detected in production; change must be made faster than normal SLA.
           Process: Eng Lead or Safety Reviewer authorises rollback/hotfix immediately;
           all other sign-offs collected retroactively within 24 hours of the change.
           Approval: retroactive. SLA: 2 hours for change, 24 hours for sign-off.

## When a Change Requires Full Re-Review
The full six-domain design review must be repeated (not just the affected domains) when:
- The feature scope changes by more than 20% (new capabilities or removed core capabilities)
- The harm category assessment changes materially
- More than 60 days elapse between original sign-off and launch
- The underlying model version changes to a different model family
The Prompt Change Policy

Publish a formal prompt change policy document for every agentic AI feature. This document is the single authoritative reference for the change control process and should be linked from the PRD and the RACI. It prevents individual team members from improvising the process under pressure.

# prompt-change-policy.md
# Governs all modifications to the approved system prompt after design sign-off.

## Policy Statement
No change to the approved system prompt, tool definitions, or feature scope
may be deployed to production without following this change control policy.
Changes made without following this policy are policy violations and must be
reported to the Safety Reviewer within 24 hours of discovery.

## Prompt Versioning Convention
All approved prompt versions are stored in:
  /prompts/approved/<feature-id>/<hash>.txt

Where <hash> is the first 8 characters of the SHA-256 of the prompt content.
The active production prompt hash is recorded in:
  /config/active-prompts.json

## Change Workflow

1. PROPOSE: Requestor files a change request in the issue tracker with:
   - Motivation: why is the change needed?
   - Classification: Minor / Major / Safety / Emergency
   - Affected domains: which RACI domains are impacted?
   - Draft change: the exact wording to be changed

2. CLASSIFY: PM reviews within 4 business hours and confirms or adjusts
   the classification. Classification determines the approval path.

3. REVIEW: Impacted domain accountables review and approve (or reject) the
   change within the SLA for its class. Rejection requires a written reason
   and an alternative proposal.

4. IMPLEMENT: Engineer applies the change to the approved prompt file,
   computes the new hash, and runs the eval suite. If the eval suite fails,
   the change does not proceed without Safety Reviewer override.

5. LOG: Change log entry is created (or updated from draft) with the new
   hash, approver names, and SLA confirmation.

6. DEPLOY: Prompt deployed to production via the standard CI/CD pipeline.
   The active-prompts.json is updated atomically with the deployment.

## Prohibited Changes
The following changes may not be made under any classification level
without a full six-domain design re-review:
- Adding autonomous financial transaction capability
- Removing a harm category from the safety impact assessment
- Granting the agent access to PII that it did not previously have
- Changing the model to a different model family (e.g. Claude to GPT)
Integrating Change Control with Git

Store every approved system prompt version in a versioned directory within the repository. The version file name is the prompt hash. The CI/CD pipeline should validate that the prompt hash deployed to production matches a hash that has an approval record in the change log.
Directory structure:
store approved prompts under `/prompts/approved/<feature-id>/`. Never store unapproved prompt drafts in this directory — use a `/prompts/drafts/` directory instead.
Hash as filename:
naming the file after its hash makes it immediately clear which version is which without reading the file. A CI check can verify that the active prompt hash matches a file in the approved directory.
Active prompt reference:

maintain a configuration file that maps feature IDs to active prompt hashes. This file is the single source of truth for what is currently deployed, and it is updated atomically as part of the deployment pipeline.
Branch protection:

the `/prompts/approved/` directory should be subject to code ownership rules (CODEOWNERS) requiring review from both the PM and the Safety Reviewer before any merge. This makes the git history a component of the change log.
Common Change Control Failures
Informal scope adjustments in standup:
verbal scope changes during engineering standups are the most common cause of design drift. Any scope change agreed verbally must be formalised in the change log within 24 hours.
Treating eval failures as optional:
teams under schedule pressure sometimes skip the eval suite run after a minor change. An eval failure on a 'minor' change is a signal that the change was misclassified.
Retroactive log entries:

writing change log entries after the fact produces incomplete records — approver names are forgotten, motivations are reconstructed rather than recorded, and the sequence of changes becomes unclear. Log before making the change.
Emergency exceptions becoming the norm:
if more than one emergency change occurs per sprint, the team is using the emergency classification to bypass the normal process. Audit emergency changes monthly and investigate patterns.
The Change Control Script

The following script provides a CLI interface for the change control process. It validates the change classification, computes the new prompt hash, runs the eval suite, records the change log entry, and updates the active prompt configuration. It replaces informal manual steps with a traceable automated workflow.

#!/usr/bin/env python3
# change_control.py
# CLI tool for managing design change control after sign-off.
# Enforces classification, records the log entry, and runs the eval suite.
# Usage: python change_control.py --feature billing-agent --type MINOR --summary "Soften refusal wording"

import argparse
import hashlib
import json
import os
import re
import subprocess
import sys
from datetime import datetime
from pathlib import Path

CHANGE_TYPES = ["MINOR", "MAJOR", "SAFETY", "EMERGENCY"]
SLA_HOURS = {"MINOR": 4, "MAJOR": 48, "SAFETY": 24, "EMERGENCY": 2}

REQUIRED_APPROVERS = {
    "MINOR":     ["pm"],
    "MAJOR":     ["pm", "eng_lead"],     # plus any affected domain leads
    "SAFETY":    ["safety", "pm"],
    "EMERGENCY": ["eng_lead"],            # retroactive full sign-off within 24h
}

# ── Prompt hash utilities ────────────────────────────────────────────────────

def compute_hash(prompt_path: str) -> str:
    with open(prompt_path, "rb") as f:
        return hashlib.sha256(f.read()).hexdigest()[:8]

def load_active_prompt(config_path: str, feature_id: str) -> str:
    with open(config_path) as f:
        cfg = json.load(f)
    return cfg.get(feature_id, {}).get("active_hash", "")

def save_active_prompt(config_path: str, feature_id: str, new_hash: str):
    with open(config_path) as f:
        cfg = json.load(f)
    if feature_id not in cfg:
        cfg[feature_id] = {}
    cfg[feature_id]["active_hash"] = new_hash
    with open(config_path, "w") as f:
        json.dump(cfg, f, indent=2)

# ── Change log utilities ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────

def load_changelog(log_path: str) -> list:
    if not os.path.exists(log_path):
        return []
    with open(log_path) as f:
        return json.load(f)

def append_change(log_path: str, entry: dict):
    log = load_changelog(log_path)
    log.append(entry)
    with open(log_path, "w") as f:
        json.dump(log, f, indent=2)

# ── Eval suite runner ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

def run_eval_suite(feature_id: str, prompt_path: str) -> bool:
    result = subprocess.run(
        ["python", "run_evals.py", "--feature", feature_id, "--prompt", prompt_path],
        capture_output=True,
        text=True,
    )
    print(result.stdout)
    if result.returncode != 0:
        print(f"EVAL FAILED:
{result.stderr}", file=sys.stderr)
        return False
    return True

# ── Classification validator ─────────────────────────────────────────────────

def validate_classification(change_type: str, summary: str) -> list:
    warnings = []
    summary_lower = summary.lower()

    # Heuristic: if summary mentions scope-related terms, MINOR is probably wrong
    scope_terms = ["add", "new", "remove", "capability", "tool", "booking", "api"]
    if change_type == "MINOR" and any(t in summary_lower for t in scope_terms):
        warnings.append(
            f"Summary '{summary}' suggests a scope change — consider MAJOR classification."
        )

    # Heuristic: safety terms should trigger SAFETY classification
    safety_terms = ["harm", "refusal", "guardrail", "hitl", "safety", "pii", "injection"]
    if change_type in ("MINOR", "MAJOR") and any(t in summary_lower for t in safety_terms):
        warnings.append(
            f"Summary '{summary}' mentions safety-related terms — consider SAFETY classification."
        )

    return warnings

# ── Main CLI ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

def main():
    parser = argparse.ArgumentParser(description="Design change control after sign-off")
    parser.add_argument("--feature",    required=True, help="Feature ID")
    parser.add_argument("--type",       required=True, choices=CHANGE_TYPES)
    parser.add_argument("--summary",    required=True, help="One-line change summary")
    parser.add_argument("--requestor",  required=True, help="GitHub username of requestor")
    parser.add_argument("--prompt",     required=True, help="Path to new prompt file")
    parser.add_argument("--domains",    default="",    help="Comma-separated impacted domains")
    parser.add_argument("--config",     default="config/active-prompts.json")
    parser.add_argument("--log",        default="design-change-log.json")
    parser.add_argument("--dry-run",    action="store_true")
    args = parser.parse_args()

    print(f"
Change Control: {args.feature} / {args.type}")
    print(f"  Summary  : {args.summary}")
    print(f"  Requestor: {args.requestor}")
    print(f"  SLA      : {SLA_HOURS[args.type]} business hours")
    print(f"  Approvers: {', '.join(REQUIRED_APPROVERS[args.type])}")

    # Validate classification
    warnings = validate_classification(args.type, args.summary)
    for w in warnings:
        print(f"  WARNING: {w}")

    # Compute new hash
    new_hash = compute_hash(args.prompt)
    old_hash = load_active_prompt(args.config, args.feature)
    print(f"  Old hash : {old_hash}")
    print(f"  New hash : {new_hash}")

    if new_hash == old_hash:
        print("  No change in prompt content — aborting.")
        sys.exit(0)

    # Run eval suite
    print("
Running eval suite...")
    eval_passed = run_eval_suite(args.feature, args.prompt)
    if not eval_passed and args.type != "EMERGENCY":
        print("Eval suite failed. Change cannot proceed without Safety Reviewer override.")
        sys.exit(1)
    if not eval_passed and args.type == "EMERGENCY":
        print("Eval suite failed but change type is EMERGENCY. Proceeding. Fix eval within 24h.")

    # Log the change
    entry = {
        "feature": args.feature,
        "type": args.type,
        "date": datetime.utcnow().isoformat() + "Z",
        "requestor": args.requestor,
        "impacted_domains": args.domains,
        "summary": args.summary,
        "old_hash": old_hash,
        "new_hash": new_hash,
        "sla_hours": SLA_HOURS[args.type],
        "required_approvers": REQUIRED_APPROVERS[args.type],
        "approvals_obtained": [],       # filled in during approval workflow
        "eval_passed": eval_passed,
    }

    if not args.dry_run:
        append_change(args.log, entry)
        save_active_prompt(args.config, args.feature, new_hash)
        print(f"
Change logged to {args.log}. Awaiting approvals from: {entry['required_approvers']}")
    else:
        print("
DRY RUN: No files written.")
        print(json.dumps(entry, indent=2))

if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()
Prompt Change Frequency as a Health Signal

The rate of post-sign-off changes is a health signal for the design process. A well-designed feature with thorough prototype testing should require few post-sign-off changes. A feature with many post-sign-off changes indicates that the design phase was not completed rigorously — the team is effectively doing design during engineering, which is expensive and risky.

Track change frequency per feature and per change class. If minor changes are frequent, the copywriting or tone calibration was underspecified in the original design. If major changes are frequent, the scope definition was premature. If safety changes are frequent, the safety impact assessment was incomplete. Each pattern points to a different upstream fix.
Target:

no more than three minor changes, one major change, and zero safety or emergency changes in the first 30 days post-launch. If this target is consistently missed, the root cause is almost always in the design phase, not in engineering.
Change velocity metric:
compute the rolling 7-day count of post-sign-off changes per feature. Alert when this exceeds three in a week — it indicates the feature is unstable and needs a design stabilisation review.
Class distribution:

if more than 20% of changes are safety-class, the safety impact assessment process needs to be strengthened. Safety changes should be rare because safety requirements should be comprehensive at design time.
Root cause review:

every major and safety change should include a retrospective question: 'what in the design phase would have prevented this change?' The answer feeds back into the design review checklist for the next feature.
Coordinating Changes Across Multiple Features

Organisations running multiple agentic AI features simultaneously may have shared components — a common system prompt prefix, a shared tool library, or a shared content filter. A change to a shared component is a change to every feature that uses it and requires the change control process to run for each affected feature.
Shared prompt prefix:

if multiple features share a common system prompt prefix (such as a company-wide AI disclosure statement or a shared safety preamble), changes to the prefix must go through the change control process for all affected features simultaneously. Use a single change log entry that lists all affected feature IDs.
Tool library updates:

changes to shared tool definitions (function signatures, return types, error handling) must be coordinated across all features that use the tool. A tool change that is MINOR for one feature may be MAJOR for another depending on how the tool is used.
Shared eval suite:

maintain a cross-feature eval suite that runs after any change to a shared component. A change that passes the per-feature eval suite but fails the cross-feature suite should be treated as a MAJOR change requiring multi-feature sign-off.
Change freeze periods:

consider instituting design change freezes in the 72 hours before a planned launch. During the freeze, only EMERGENCY changes are permitted. This prevents last-minute scope adjustments from destabilising a carefully tested feature.
Documentation After Each Change

Every post-sign-off change must be reflected in four places: the change log, the active prompt configuration, the PRD, and the iteration log. Updating only the change log and the prompt produces documentation debt that compounds over time — a PRD that no longer matches the deployed system is worse than no PRD at all, because it actively misleads anyone who reads it.
PRD scope section:
if a major change adds or removes a capability, the PRD scope section must be updated within 24 hours of the change being approved. The PRD version number should reflect the change.
System prompt baseline in RACI:
the approved prompt hash in the stakeholder sign-off RACI must be updated to match the new hash. An RACI that references an old hash is out of date and must not be used for compliance purposes.
Iteration log:

the iteration log entry for the change should include the motivation (what was observed), the hypothesis (what the change tests), and the outcome (what the eval suite showed). This preserves the reasoning chain across team transitions.
